skill.name: python-best-practices
skill.description: Guides Python development with type-first patterns, structured configuration, explicit errors, and practical type-checking workflows.
skill.summary: Apply type-first Python patterns for clearer models, safer configuration, and maintainable modules.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a high score because Markdown code fences, inline backticks, environment-variable examples, and a documentation link matched generic patterns. Review found no executable skill code, no prompt injection, and no evidence of malicious intent. Residual risk is low because the guidance includes optional external tool commands and examples that reference secret-bearing environment variables.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick alerts point to Markdown code fences, inline Python identifiers, and explanatory examples. The only actual shell commands are optional ty usage examples, not automatic execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown guidance and the flagged regions are code blocks or inline formatting. The command examples require a user or agent to choose to run them.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Reference Secrets Without Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The configuration example reads DATABASE_URL and API_KEY from os.environ. This is legitimate application configuration guidance, and no network transmission or logging of these values is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippet demonstrates normal startup configuration loading. It does not send, print, store, or transform secrets outside the process.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Documentation Link
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to public documentation for the ty type checker. There is no fetch, curl, tracking endpoint, or data transfer in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only as a Markdown hyperlink. No executable network client or credential-bearing request is present.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Alert Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static role-token finding maps to a Python enum branch using Role.USER. It is not a command to the assistant and does not attempt to override instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is an Enum example for permission checks. There are no phrases such as ignore instructions, system update, or override commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance detections point to ordinary prose and Python practice examples. No hashing algorithm, encryption routine, host enumeration, or system survey behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found type-system guidance, exception handling, and configuration advice at these lines. No security-sensitive primitive or system enumeration command appears there.

low_findings.title: Critical Dangerous Combination Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The heuristic combines unrelated Markdown examples: shell command examples, environment configuration examples, and one documentation hyperlink. No single flow combines execution, networking, and credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious elements are separated educational examples in a Markdown document. There is no code path, agent instruction, or data flow linking secrets to network or command execution.

content.user_title: Improve Python Code With Type-First Patterns
content.value_statement: Python projects often drift into unclear models, scattered configuration, and weak error handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for safer Python changes.
actual_capabilities: Recommends dataclasses, TypedDict, NewType, Literal unions, and Protocol for Python design.
Guides module organization with focused files, public APIs, and colocated tests.
Promotes explicit exception handling, context preservation, and boundary-case coverage.
Shows a typed configuration pattern using environment variables and startup validation.
Suggests ty, pyright, or mypy for Python type-checking workflows.
limitations: It is guidance-only and does not include executable automation or validators.
It focuses on general Python patterns, not framework-specific architecture.
The ty recommendation may change because the tool is described as early stage.
Security guidance is limited to safe configuration and explicit failure patterns.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Python Module
use_cases.description: Apply type-first structure before changing implementation details in an existing module.

use_cases.title: Review Configuration Handling
use_cases.description: Replace scattered environment access with a typed configuration object and startup validation.

use_cases.title: Strengthen Code Review Standards
use_cases.description: Use consistent expectations for types, errors, tests, and module boundaries during review.

prompt_templates.title: Add Type Hints
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner update to a small Python function or module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python file and suggest type-first improvements using dataclasses, TypedDict, or clearer function signatures where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Model Domain Data
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate design for structured application data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design Python data models for this feature using immutable dataclasses, NewType identifiers, and constrained values where they improve correctness.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Error Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of reliability and debugging behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python logic for swallowed exceptions, missing edge cases, and unclear failure paths. Propose explicit error handling and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Typed Refactor
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced refactor across modules and tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a refactor plan that introduces typed configuration, Protocol boundaries, focused modules, and type-checking without changing behavior.

output_examples.input: A Python service reads environment variables throughout several modules.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a single typed configuration object, startup validation, and explicit production requirements for secrets.

output_examples.input: A function accepts plain strings for different identifier types.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests domain-specific identifier types so callers cannot mix unrelated values accidentally.

output_examples.input: A review asks whether a large module should be split.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies separate concerns, proposes smaller modules, and keeps tests aligned with the new structure.

best_practices: Define data shapes and function signatures before changing implementation logic.
Validate external input and configuration at system boundaries.
Add focused tests when modifying behavior or error handling.
anti_patterns: Do not scatter environment variable reads across many modules.
Do not swallow exceptions without preserving useful context.
Do not rely on plain strings when domain-specific types would prevent mixups.

faq.question: Does this skill run Python tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Any command execution requires a separate user or agent action.

faq.question: Which Python typing features does it emphasize?
faq.answer: It emphasizes dataclasses, TypedDict, NewType, Literal unions, Enum, Protocol, and explicit function signatures.

faq.question: Is this only for new projects?
faq.answer: No. It can guide refactors, code reviews, configuration cleanup, and incremental typing in existing projects.

faq.question: Does it replace mypy or pyright?
faq.answer: No. It suggests type-checking workflows and mentions ty, pyright, and mypy as tool choices.

faq.question: Does it include framework-specific advice?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is general Python practice and should be adapted to each framework.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked low?
faq.answer: The file is documentation-only, but it includes optional shell commands and examples that reference environment secrets.
